abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a core approach for robot control when expert demonstrations are unavailable. On-policy methods such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) are widely used for their stability, but their reliance on narrowly distributed on-policy data limits accurate policy evaluation in high-dimensional state and action spaces. Off-policy methods can overcome this limitation by learning from a broader state-action distribution, yet suffer from slow convergence and instability, as fitting a value function over diverse data requires many gradient updates, causing critic errors to accumulate through bootstrapping. We present FlashSAC, a fast and stable off-policy RL algorithm built on Soft Actor-Critic. Motivated by scaling laws observed in supervised learning, FlashSAC sharply reduces gradient updates while compensating with larger models and higher data throughput. To maintain stability at increased scale, FlashSAC explicitly bounds weight, feature, and gradient norms, curbing critic error accumulation. Across over 60 tasks in 10 simulators, FlashSAC consistently outperforms PPO and strong off-policy baselines in both final performance and training efficiency, with the largest gains on high-dimensional tasks such as dexterous manipulation. In sim-to-real humanoid locomotion, FlashSAC reduces training time from hours to minutes, demonstrating the promise of off-policy RL for sim-to-real transfer.

citations.fullCitation: [20] Tuomas Haarnoja, Aurick Zhou, Pieter Abbeel, and Sergey Levine. Soft actor-critic: Off-policy maximum entropy deep reinforcement learning with a stochastic actor. In International conference on machine learning, pages 1861–1870. PMLR, 2018.
citations.justification: The main paper explicitly states that FlashSAC is built upon the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm. This citation introduces the foundational off-policy, maximum-entropy framework that FlashSAC directly extends to improve speed and stability in high-dimensional settings.
citations.title: Soft actor-critic: Off-policy maximum entropy deep reinforcement learning with a stochastic actor

citations.fullCitation: [72] John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, and Oleg Klimov. Proximal policy optimization algorithms. arXiv preprint arXiv:1707.06347, 2017.
citations.justification: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is the primary on-policy baseline that FlashSAC is compared against throughout the paper. The authors frame their work as a solution to the data inefficiency and scaling limitations of stable on-policy methods like PPO, making it a critical point of comparison.
citations.title: Proximal policy optimization algorithms

citations.fullCitation: [32] Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Henighan, Tom B Brown, Benjamin Chess, Rewon Child, Scott Gray, Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, and Dario Amodei. Scaling laws for neural language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.08361, 2020.
citations.justification: This paper provides the core motivation for one of FlashSAC's key design principles. The authors explicitly state their strategy of reducing gradient updates while increasing model size and data throughput is inspired by the scaling trends observed in supervised learning, as established by this work.
citations.title: Scaling laws for neural language models

citations.fullCitation: [64] Daniel Palenicek, Florian Vogt, Joe Watson, Ingmar Posner, and Jan Peters. XQC: Well-conditioned optimization accelerates deep reinforcement learning. International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR), 2026.
citations.justification: XQC is a state-of-the-art off-policy method focused on stabilizing training through well-conditioned optimization, which is a central theme in FlashSAC's design. FlashSAC integrates similar stabilization techniques, such as batch normalization, and uses XQC as a key performance baseline in its experiments.
citations.title: XQC: Well-conditioned optimization accelerates deep reinforcement learning

citations.fullCitation: [15] Scott Fujimoto, Herke Hoof, and David Meger. Addressing function approximation error in actor-critic methods. In International conference on machine learning, pages 1587–1596. PMLR, 2018.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Twin Delayed DDPG (TD3), which addresses the critical issue of value function overestimation in off-policy learning. Its techniques, particularly clipped double Q-learning, were incorporated into SAC and thus form a fundamental building block for the stability of modern actor-critic methods like FlashSAC.
citations.title: Addressing function approximation error in actor-critic methods

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "FlashSAC: Fast and Stable Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for High-Dimensional Robot Control."

---

### Research Paper Report: FlashSAC

**1. Authors and Institution(s)**

The research was conducted by a collaborative team of authors affiliated with several institutions:
*   **Donghu Kim**: Holiday Robotics, KAIST
*   **Youngdo Lee**: KAIST, KRAFTON
*   **Minho Park**: KAIST
*   **Kinam Kim**: KAIST
*   **I Made Aswin Nahendra**: KRAFTON
*   **Takuma Seno**: Turing Inc
*   **Sehee Min**: Holiday Robotics
*   **Daniel Palenicek**: TU Darmstadt, hessian.AI
*   **Florian Vogt**: KTH Royal Institute of Technology
*   **Danica Kragic**: KTH Royal Institute of Technology
*   **Jan Peters**: TU Darmstadt, hessian.AI, German Research Center for AI (DFKI), Robotics Institute Germany (RIG)
*   **Jaegul Choo**: KAIST
*   **Hojoon Lee**: Holiday Robotics, KAIST

Donghu Kim and Youngdo Lee are noted as having contributed equally, while Jaegul Choo and Hojoon Lee are joint corresponding authors.

**2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape**

The field of robot learning aims to develop agents capable of generalizing across diverse real-world tasks. Reinforcement Learning (RL) from simulation is a fundamental approach in scenarios where expert demonstrations are not available or are insufficient. Historically, sim-to-real RL has shown success in constrained domains like quadruped locomotion and gripper-based manipulation, which feature low-dimensional state-action spaces and high-throughput simulators. In this context, on-policy methods, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), have been widely utilized due to their stability and ease of tuning, with their data inefficiency being manageable when data collection is inexpensive.

However, the contemporary landscape of robotics is evolving towards more complex applications, including humanoid locomotion, dexterous manipulation, and vision-based control. These applications involve significantly higher-dimensional state and action spaces. In such environments, policy evaluation and improvement from the narrowly distributed data typical of on-policy rollouts become challenging. Additionally, simulation itself can become more computationally intensive, and larger policy architectures increase rollout costs. This shift renders the practice of repeatedly discarding past experience, a characteristic of on-policy methods, increasingly inefficient in terms of both sample complexity and wall-clock time.

Off-policy RL presents an alternative by enabling the reuse of diverse experience stored in a replay buffer, thereby promising higher data efficiency. This characteristic is particularly attractive for high-dimensional robotic tasks, as broader data coverage can support more accurate policy evaluation and improvement. Despite this potential, off-policy RL has not become the default for sim-to-real transfer, primarily due to issues of slow convergence and training instability. A central difficulty lies in learning an accurate value function (critic) from diverse replay data. This process often necessitates numerous gradient updates, which extends training time and exacerbates the accumulation of estimation errors through bootstrapping—where the critic is optimized towards targets that depend on its own predictions.

Prior research has addressed aspects of this challenge in isolation. Some efforts have focused on improving training speed by scaling data throughput through parallel simulation and large replay buffers. For example, FastTD3 demonstrated strong wall-clock efficiency but relied on small network architectures to mitigate instability, which inherently limited its asymptotic performance. Scaling to larger networks in these approaches typically amplifies instability under bootstrapped training. Another line of research has concentrated on enhancing stability by constraining value-function sensitivity through methods such as bounding feature, weight, or gradient norms (e.g., XQC, SimbaV2). While these techniques allow for training with larger networks and achieving higher asymptotic performance, they often require more gradient updates for convergence, leading to slower training in data-rich simulation environments.

FlashSAC positions itself within this research context by aiming to synthesize these two approaches. It seeks to achieve rapid training through a reduced number of gradient updates, compensating by employing larger models and increased data throughput, while simultaneously maintaining stability by explicitly bounding weight, feature, and gradient norms to prevent the accumulation of critic errors. The algorithm builds upon the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) framework, a widely used off-policy RL algorithm known for its entropy regularization.

**3. Key Objectives and Motivation**

The primary objective of this work is to develop a fast and stable off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm, named FlashSAC, tailored for high-dimensional robotic control tasks. This overarching goal is motivated by several identified limitations within the current landscape of robot learning:

*   **Addressing On-Policy Limitations in High-Dimensional Control:** Traditional on-policy methods like PPO, while stable and easy to implement, suffer from inherent data inefficiency. This inefficiency becomes a critical bottleneck in high-dimensional robotic tasks (e.g., humanoid locomotion, dexterous manipulation, vision-based control) where achieving sufficient state-action space coverage through on-policy rollouts is prohibitively expensive. As simulation costs rise, repeatedly discarding collected data becomes increasingly impractical, necessitating more data-efficient learning paradigms.
*   **Overcoming Off-Policy Challenges (Speed and Stability):** While off-policy methods inherently offer data efficiency by reusing past experiences from a replay buffer, their widespread adoption for sim-to-real transfer has been hindered by two persistent challenges:
    *   **Slow Training:** Learning an accurate value function from diverse off-policy data, especially in high-dimensional state-action spaces, typically requires a substantial number of gradient updates. This contributes directly to longer wall-clock training times.
    *   **Instability:** The bootstrapped Bellman objective, fundamental to off-policy value function learning, can lead to compounding estimation errors. Approximation and extrapolation errors from poorly supported state-action pairs can propagate and amplify across successive updates, resulting in unstable training dynamics, particularly when using larger model capacities.
*   **Bridging the Gap in Existing Off-Policy Solutions:** Previous attempts to enhance off-policy RL often tackled speed and stability in isolation. Approaches prioritizing speed typically used smaller networks to prevent instability, thus limiting asymptotic performance. Conversely, methods focused on stability employed techniques like norm constraints to enable larger networks and higher performance, but at the cost of requiring more updates and thus slower training. FlashSAC aims to unify these objectives by developing a system that is both fast *and* stable.
*   **Enabling Scalable Sim-to-Real Transfer:** The ultimate motivation is to make off-policy RL a reliable and efficient choice for sim-to-real robotic learning. By accelerating training and enhancing stability, FlashSAC seeks to reduce the time required to develop and deploy effective policies on physical robots, particularly for complex, high-dimensional systems where on-policy methods are currently dominant but resource-intensive.

In summary, FlashSAC aims to achieve strong asymptotic performance with significantly reduced wall-clock training time by critically re-evaluating the trade-offs between model scale, data throughput, and update frequency, while simultaneously integrating robust mechanisms to ensure training stability under these aggressive scaling conditions.

**4. Methodology and Approach**

FlashSAC is an off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm built upon the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) framework. Its design integrates three primary mechanisms: fast training through scaled data and model capacity with reduced gradient updates, stable training via constrained critic update dynamics, and broad exploration strategies.

**4.1. Algorithmic Foundation: Soft Actor-Critic (SAC)**
FlashSAC leverages SAC's core components:
*   **Replay Buffer:** Stores transitions (s, a, r, s') collected from past policies.
*   **Entropy Regularization:** Encourages exploration by maximizing expected return in addition to policy entropy. This is important for covering high-dimensional state-action spaces.
*   **Clipped Double Q-learning:** Utilizes two action-value functions, Q_phi1 and Q_phi2, taking the minimum of their estimates for target formation, which helps mitigate optimistic value errors.
*   **Target Networks:** Employs slowly updated target networks (exponential moving average) for Q-value functions to stabilize bootstrapping.

**4.2. Fast Training Mechanisms**
Inspired by scaling laws in supervised learning, FlashSAC aims to reduce gradient updates while increasing model size and data throughput.
*   **Massively Parallel Simulation:** Data collection is performed using 1024 parallel simulation environments, facilitating rapid accumulation of diverse trajectories. This contrasts with setups that use fewer environments.
*   **Large-Capacity Replay Buffer:** A replay buffer of up to 10 million transitions is used. This is an order of magnitude larger than typically employed, intended to preserve rare but important state-action pairs and maintain data diversity in high-dimensional tasks, mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
*   **Large Model, Large Batch, Fewer Updates:**
    *   **Model Size:** Both actor and critic networks feature 2.5 million parameters and 6 layers, substantially larger than the 0.2-0.5 million parameters and 2-3 layers often used in conventional off-policy methods.
    *   **Batch Size:** A batch size of 2048 is employed to maximize GPU utilization.
    *   **Updates-to-Data (UTD) Ratio:** A low UTD ratio of 2/1024 is set, meaning only 2 gradient updates are performed for every 1024 new transitions. This infrequent updating strategy is supported by the combination of large batches, higher learning rates, and increased model capacity.
*   **Code Optimization:** The implementation uses PyTorch with JIT compilation for reduced Python overhead and mixed-precision training for a 5-10% reduction in wall-clock time.

**4.3. Stable Training Mechanisms**
To counter instability from bootstrapped critic updates exacerbated by large models and high data throughput, FlashSAC incorporates explicit control over critic update dynamics.
*   **Inverted Residual Backbone:** The network backbone consists of stacked inverted residual blocks, similar to Transformer feedforward blocks. These blocks expand features to a higher dimension, project them back, and use residual connections for stable gradient propagation.
*   **Pre-activation Batch Normalization:** Batch normalization is applied before each nonlinearity. This is chosen over layer normalization to leverage large-batch statistics from diverse replay data, aiming for a smoother loss landscape and better handling of non-stationary input distributions from evolving policies.
*   **Cross-Batch Value Prediction:** Current and next-state transitions are concatenated into a single batch. This ensures that both receive identical batch normalization statistics, maintaining consistency in the Bellman update process.
*   **Distributional Critic with Adaptive Reward Scaling:** Q-values are represented as a categorical distribution over `n_atom` atoms. The network predicts atom probabilities, and training uses a cross-entropy loss against a projected Bellman target. To keep returns within the critic's fixed support, rewards are adaptively scaled based on the running discounted return variance and maximum magnitude.
*   **Weight Normalization:** After each gradient step, weight vectors are projected onto a unit-norm sphere, and normalization parameters are constrained to a norm of `sqrt(d)`. This prevents uncontrolled weight growth, which can increase Q-value variance and error amplification.

**4.4. Exploration Strategies**
FlashSAC employs two complementary mechanisms to achieve broad state-action coverage.
*   **Unified Entropy Target:** Instead of per-task entropy tuning, the target entropy for maximum-entropy RL is parameterized by a fixed action standard deviation (σ_tgt = 0.15). This scales linearly with the action dimension, providing consistent exploration across different embodiments without manual tuning.
*   **Noise Repetition:** To induce temporal correlation in actions, a noise vector `ϵ ~ N(0, I)` is sampled and held constant for `k` consecutive steps. The repetition length `k` is drawn from a Zeta distribution, favoring short repeats but occasionally generating longer correlated sequences. This is designed as a lightweight alternative to traditional correlated noise processes for massively parallel simulations.

**5. Main Findings and Results**

FlashSAC was extensively evaluated across more than 60 locomotion and manipulation tasks within 10 different simulators, encompassing state-based (low and high-dimensional), vision-based, and sim-to-real control scenarios. Performance was assessed in terms of both asymptotic return and wall-clock training efficiency.

**5.1. State-Based RL on GPU-based Simulators (IsaacLab, MuJoCo Playground, ManiSkill, Genesis)**
*   **Low-dimensional tasks:** On tasks such as gripper manipulation and quadruped locomotion, FlashSAC achieved performance comparable to PPO. This indicates that in settings with small state-action spaces and high simulation throughput, on-policy methods remain effective.
*   **High-dimensional tasks:** For tasks involving dexterous manipulation (e.g., Allegro, Shadow Hand) and humanoid locomotion (e.g., Unitree G1, H1, Booster T1), FlashSAC demonstrated a consistent and clear advantage. It converged reliably to higher asymptotic performance while requiring substantially less wall-clock time compared to PPO.
*   **Comparison to FastTD3:** FlashSAC exhibited greater stability than FastTD3, successfully converging on tasks where FastTD3 frequently failed or underperformed. In cases where both methods converged, FlashSAC achieved higher asymptotic performance, with the benefits of its larger model capacity being particularly evident in humanoid locomotion tasks.

**5.2. State-Based RL on CPU-based Simulators (Gym-MuJoCo, DeepMind Control Suite, HumanoidBench, MyoSuite)**
*   In environments where sample efficiency is paramount due to single-environment, CPU-based simulation, FlashSAC consistently outperformed PPO and other strong sample-efficient baselines, including off-policy methods (XQC, SimbaV2, MR.Q) and model-based methods (TD-MPC2).
*   PPO showed particularly poor performance in this regime, highlighting its data inefficiency under limited sample budgets.
*   These results indicated that FlashSAC's design principles generalize effectively beyond massively parallel GPU-based simulation, matching or exceeding specialized sample-efficient methods without requiring task-specific hyperparameter tuning.

**5.3. Vision-based RL (DMControl Suite)**
*   On 8 vision-based tasks from the DMControl Suite, FlashSAC matched or exceeded baselines such as DrQ-v2 and MR.Q in asymptotic performance while achieving faster convergence in wall-clock time.
*   DrQ-v2, while sample-efficient, exhibited instability and failed to converge on several tasks. MR.Q achieved high final performance but with additional computational overhead due to its auxiliary dynamics model. FlashSAC achieved competitive or superior results with a single set of hyperparameters, without the need for task-specific exploration or auxiliary objectives.

**5.4. Sim-to-Real Transfer (Unitree G1 Humanoid Locomotion)**
*   **Flat Terrain:** FlashSAC enabled stable real-world locomotion for a 29-DoF Unitree G1 humanoid after approximately 20 minutes of training in simulation. This contrasted with PPO, which required about 3 hours to achieve comparable performance.
*   **Rough Terrain (Stairs):** On a challenging stair-climbing task with conditions unseen during training, FlashSAC successfully climbed 15cm stairs after approximately 4 hours of training. PPO required nearly 20 hours to reach a similar capability.
*   Overall, FlashSAC demonstrated a reduction in training time for sim-to-real humanoid locomotion by nearly an order of magnitude compared to PPO, while maintaining stable and safe real-world behaviors.

**5.5. Analysis and Ablation Studies**
*   **Data Coverage:** Off-policy data collected in FlashSAC's replay buffer was shown to cover a substantially broader region of the state-action space compared to on-policy data, supporting the hypothesis that wider data coverage contributes to improved policy evaluation in high-dimensional tasks.
*   **Scaling Ablation:** Increasing replay buffer size up to 10M transitions improved performance and stability, although excessively large buffers (50M) slowed learning. Scaling batch size and network capacity (width, depth) while reducing the updates-to-data ratio consistently accelerated convergence, affirming the applicability of scaling laws in this off-policy RL context.
*   **Architectural Ablation:** Incremental additions of residual blocks, batch normalization, RMSNorm, distributional critics with reward scaling, and weight normalization progressively improved task performance and optimization stability. These additions led to bounded parameter, feature, and gradient norms, and reduced the condition number of the critic loss landscape, indicating more well-behaved critic updates.
*   **Exploration Ablation:** The unified entropy target (σ_tgt=0.15) proved robust across tasks, simplifying hyperparameter tuning. Noise repetition was shown to be crucial for accelerating convergence and achieving higher asymptotic performance, facilitating coherent exploratory trajectories.

**6. Significance and Potential Impact**

FlashSAC represents an advancement in the field of reinforcement learning for robotics by addressing fundamental limitations that have restricted the widespread adoption of off-policy methods in complex, high-dimensional control tasks.

*   **Enabling Scalable Off-Policy RL:** The work successfully demonstrates that off-policy RL can be simultaneously fast and stable, challenging the conventional trade-off between speed and stability that has characterized previous approaches. By unifying strategies for rapid training (large models, high data throughput, reduced updates) with robust stabilization techniques (norm constraints, architectural design, distributional critics), FlashSAC provides a framework capable of handling the demands of modern robotics.
*   **Accelerated Robot Learning and Development:** A key impact is the significant reduction in wall-clock training time for intricate robotic tasks, particularly demonstrated in sim-to-real humanoid locomotion where training time was reduced by nearly an order of magnitude. This acceleration can expedite the development and deployment cycles for robotic systems, making RL-based control more practical for real-world applications.
*   **Broader Applicability for Advanced Robotics:** As robotic applications increasingly involve high-dimensional state-action spaces, rich sensory inputs (e.g., vision, tactile), and complex contact dynamics, the data inefficiency and stability issues of traditional methods become more pronounced. FlashSAC's demonstrated performance across a diverse benchmark suite—including dexterous manipulation, humanoid locomotion, and vision-based control—suggests its suitability for these emerging challenges, potentially establishing off-policy RL as a default paradigm for such tasks.
*   **Robustness and Generalization:** The ability of FlashSAC to perform effectively across a wide range of environments (GPU-based vs. CPU-based simulators, low-dimensional vs. high-dimensional tasks) and against various baselines, often with a single unified hyperparameter configuration, highlights its robustness and generalizability. This reduces the burden of task-specific tuning, a common challenge in RL research.
*   **New Research Avenues:** The stabilization principles developed in FlashSAC open opportunities for future research. This includes training even larger policies, effectively integrating multimodal sensory inputs beyond state and vision, and leveraging more realistic but computationally intensive simulators. Furthermore, off-policy methods naturally support learning from diverse data sources, such as combinations of expert demonstrations and self-collected experience. The paper also points to extending these critic-stabilization principles to tactile-based learning as a promising direction.

In summary, FlashSAC offers a computationally efficient and stable off-policy reinforcement learning framework that can potentially unlock new capabilities in high-dimensional robotic control and accelerate the transition of learned policies from simulation to real-world deployment.
overview:
The advancement of robotic control relies heavily on simulation-to-real (sim-to-real) transfer, where agents are trained in virtual environments before being deployed on physical hardware. In this domain, on-policy methods like Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) have become the standard because of their stability and reliability. However, as robotics moves toward high-dimensional systems—such as humanoid robots and dexterous robotic hands—on-policy methods face significant challenges due to their data inefficiency. These methods require a vast number of interactions and typically discard past experiences, leading to long training times.

Off-policy reinforcement learning (RL) offers a more efficient alternative by reusing past data stored in a replay buffer. Despite this promise, off-policy methods have struggled with training instability and slow wall-clock convergence in high-dimensional tasks. **FlashSAC** is an off-policy algorithm designed to bridge this gap, achieving both rapid training and robust stability for complex robotic control.

![FlashSAC Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04539v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: FlashSAC demonstrates high-performance and fast convergence across various robotic tasks, including low-dimensional gripper manipulation, high-dimensional humanoid locomotion, and direct sim-to-real transfer.*

## The Challenge of Off-Policy Stability

The core of off-policy learning often relies on the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) framework. SAC aims to maximize a combination of expected return and the entropy of the policy, which encourages exploration. The objective function $J(\pi)$ is defined as:

$$
J(\pi) = \sum_{t=0}^T \mathbb{E}_{(s_t, a_t) \sim \rho_\pi} [r(s_t, a_t) + \alpha \mathcal{H}(\pi(\cdot|s_t))]
$$

where $r$ is the reward, $\alpha$ is the entropy temperature, and $\mathcal{H}$ represents the entropy. To optimize this, the agent learns a value function (critic) $Q_\phi$ that estimates the expected return of taking action $a$ in state $s$. This is done using a bootstrapped Bellman target $y$:

$$
y = r + \gamma (Q_{\bar{\phi}}(s', a') - \alpha \log \pi(a'|s'))
$$

In high-dimensional spaces, learning an accurate critic is difficult. Errors in value estimation can accumulate through the bootstrapping process, where the critic is updated based on its own (potentially incorrect) future predictions. While prior work has tried to speed up training by using many parallel environments, doing so often causes the critic to diverge if the model capacity is large or the update frequency is high.

## Scaling for Speed

FlashSAC adopts a different strategy for scaling. Instead of performing many small gradient updates on a small network, it uses a large model and performs fewer, more informative updates on massive batches of data.

1.  **Massively Parallel Simulation:** FlashSAC utilizes 1024 parallel simulation environments, allowing for rapid data collection.
2.  **Large-Capacity Model:** The actor and critic networks are significantly larger than traditional off-policy models, featuring approximately 2.5 million parameters across 6 layers. This increased capacity allows the model to capture the nuances of high-dimensional state-action spaces.
3.  **Low Updates-to-Data (UTD) Ratio:** Traditional methods might perform one or more gradient updates for every environment step. FlashSAC uses a very low UTD ratio of $2/1024$. This means for every 1024 steps collected across the parallel environments, only 2 gradient updates are performed. This strategy maximizes the utility of each update by using a large batch size of 2048, significantly reducing wall-clock training time.
4.  **Expansive Replay Buffer:** To prevent catastrophic forgetting and maintain data diversity, FlashSAC employs a replay buffer of up to 10 million transitions. This ensures that the agent can still learn from rare but successful experiences encountered early in training.

## Architecting for Stability

To ensure that the large model remains stable despite aggressive scaling and off-policy data, FlashSAC incorporates several architectural and optimization constraints.

![FlashSAC Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04539v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The FlashSAC critic utilizes an inverted residual backbone with pre-activation batch normalization and RMSNorm to stabilize gradient flow and bound feature magnitudes.*

### Backbone Design
The critic uses a backbone composed of stacked inverted residual blocks. This design expands features into a higher-dimensional space before projecting them back, which, combined with residual connections, ensures stable gradient propagation.

### Normalization Techniques
FlashSAC uses a combination of Batch Normalization (BN) and RMSNorm. Specifically, pre-activation BN is used to handle the non-stationary data distributions typical of RL. To maintain consistency during the Bellman update, current states and next states are processed in a single concatenated batch (**Cross-Batch Value Prediction**), ensuring they share the same batch statistics.

### Distributional Critic and Reward Scaling
Rather than predicting a single scalar value, the critic learns a categorical distribution over $n_{\text{atom}}$ atoms. This distributional approach provides a richer signal for learning and has been shown to improve stability. To keep the value estimates within the critic's fixed support, rewards are adaptively scaled based on the running variance of the discounted returns.

### Weight Normalization
To prevent uncontrolled growth in the network's weights—a common precursor to training divergence—FlashSAC applies an explicit weight normalization. After each gradient step, weight vectors are projected back onto a unit-norm sphere. This constraint limits the sensitivity of the $Q$-function to small changes in input, effectively bounding the estimation error.

## Exploration Strategies

High-dimensional control requires effective exploration to discover optimal behaviors. FlashSAC simplifies and enhances exploration through two primary mechanisms:

*   **Unified Entropy Target:** Instead of manual tuning for every new robot or task, FlashSAC uses a target entropy based on a fixed action standard deviation $\sigma_{\text{tgt}} = 0.15$. This target scales naturally with the number of action dimensions.
*   **Noise Repetition:** To create more coherent exploratory movements, the algorithm samples a noise vector $\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)$ and repeats it for $k$ steps, where $k$ is sampled from a Zeta distribution. This creates temporally correlated noise, which helps the robot explore continuous trajectories rather than jittering in place.

## Empirical Performance and Results

The efficacy of FlashSAC was evaluated across over 60 tasks in various simulators, including IsaacLab, MuJoCo, and ManiSkill.

![Isaac Performance](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04539v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: FlashSAC outperforms PPO and FastTD3 in both speed and final performance on complex tasks such as Shadow Hand manipulation and Unitree G1 locomotion.*

### High-Dimensional Control
On tasks involving 20+ degrees of freedom (DoF), such as the Allegro and Shadow robotic hands or the Unitree G1 and H1 humanoids, FlashSAC consistently converged faster than PPO. In many cases, it achieved higher asymptotic returns, demonstrating that its large-scale architecture effectively handles complexity that on-policy methods struggle to navigate efficiently.

### Sim-to-Real Transfer
A critical test for FlashSAC was the deployment on a physical Unitree G1 humanoid robot.

*   **Efficiency:** FlashSAC achieved stable locomotion on flat terrain after only 20 minutes of training, compared to 3 hours required by PPO.
*   **Capability:** For the more difficult task of climbing 15cm stairs (a condition not explicitly modeled in the training terrain), FlashSAC reached the required performance in 4 hours, whereas PPO took nearly 20 hours.

![G1 Stair Climbing](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04539v1/x7.png)
*Figure 4: Real-world deployment of a Unitree G1 humanoid trained with FlashSAC. The robot successfully traverses rough terrain and climbs stairs after approximately 4 hours of training.*

### Generalization
FlashSAC also showed strong performance in vision-based RL and CPU-based simulations. In these environments, where sample efficiency is the primary bottleneck, FlashSAC matched or exceeded the performance of specialized algorithms like TD-MPC2 and DrQ-v2 without requiring task-specific hyperparameter adjustments.

## Analysis of Stability Components

The success of FlashSAC stems from the cumulative effect of its stability mechanisms. Ablation studies (removing one component at a time) reveal that without weight normalization or distributional RL, the critic's gradient norms and condition numbers increase significantly, leading to unstable training and lower scores.

![Stability Ablation](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04539v1/x10.png)
*Figure 5: Ablation studies show that each component—Inverted Residuals, BatchNorm, Distributional RL, and Weight Normalization—contributes to lower gradient norms and a better-conditioned loss landscape, resulting in higher final performance.*

By combining these stabilization techniques, FlashSAC can maintain a well-behaved loss landscape even when using large networks and high-throughput data streams.

## Conclusion

FlashSAC addresses the long-standing trade-off between speed and stability in off-policy reinforcement learning for robotics. By leveraging massive parallelism, large model capacity, and a suite of stabilization techniques, it achieves convergence times that are often an order of magnitude faster than traditional on-policy methods. Its success across diverse simulations and real-world humanoid locomotion tasks suggests that stabilized off-policy learning is a viable and efficient path forward for the next generation of high-dimensional robotic control.

keyInsights: Scaling laws observed in supervised learning can be effectively applied to off-policy RL, where increasing model capacity and data throughput with reduced gradient updates leads to faster convergence, provided stability is maintained.

Robust critic stabilization through architectural designs (e.g., inverted residual blocks, pre-activation batch norm) and norm constraints is essential to prevent error accumulation and ensure stable training in high-dimensional, data-rich off-policy settings.
Broad and efficient exploration in high-dimensional spaces can be facilitated by a unified entropy target and lightweight noise repetition, avoiding complex task-specific tuning.
originalProblem: Traditional on-policy reinforcement learning methods are data inefficient for complex, high-dimensional robotic control tasks, leading to prohibitive training costs.

Off-policy reinforcement learning, despite its data efficiency potential, often faces challenges of slow convergence and training instability, particularly when dealing with large models and bootstrapped value functions.
Prior off-policy approaches struggled to simultaneously achieve both rapid training speed and robust stability, especially in environments demanding high model capacity.
results: FlashSAC reduced the wall-clock training time for sim-to-real humanoid locomotion by nearly an order of magnitude compared to PPO, achieving stable real-world behaviors.

Consistently attained higher asymptotic performance and faster convergence across more than 60 locomotion and manipulation tasks in diverse simulators, including dexterous manipulation and vision-based control.
Outperformed or matched other state-of-the-art off-policy and model-based methods across a wide range of benchmarks, demonstrating robustness with a single set of hyperparameters.
solution: Introduced FlashSAC, an off-policy algorithm built on Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), integrating mechanisms for both rapid and stable learning.

Achieves fast training by leveraging massively parallel simulation, a 10 million transition replay buffer, larger networks (2.5M parameters), large batch sizes (2048), and a low updates-to-data ratio.

Ensures stability through a suite of techniques including an inverted residual network backbone, pre-activation batch normalization, cross-batch value prediction, a distributional critic with adaptive reward scaling, and weight normalization.
summary.summary: FlashSAC is an off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm designed for high-dimensional robotic control, integrating rapid training via scaled data throughput and model capacity with stability mechanisms like constrained critic updates. The algorithm consistently reduces wall-clock training time by up to an order of magnitude and achieves higher asymptotic performance across over 60 locomotion and manipulation tasks, including successful sim-to-real humanoid locomotion.

title: FlashSAC: Fast and Stable Off-Policy Reinforcement Learning for High-Dimensional Robot Control
